title: Hot-summer humid continental climate

infobox.value: is a [climatic](/wiki/Climate "Climate") region defined by Russo-German [climatologist](/wiki/Climatologist "Climatologist") [Wladimir Köppen](/wiki/Wladimir_K%C3%B6ppen "Wladimir Köppen") in 1900,[[1]](#cite_note-2014revisit-1) typified by four distinct seasons and large seasonal temperature differences, with warm to hot (and often humid) summers, and cold (sometimes severely cold in the northern areas) and snowy winters. Precipitation is usually distributed throughout the year, but often these regions do have dry seasons. The definition of this climate in terms of temperature is as follows: the mean temperature of the coldest month must be below 0 °C (32.0 °F) or −3 °C (26.6 °F) depending on the isotherm,[[2]](#cite_note-kottek2006-2) and there must be at least four months whose mean temperatures are at or above 10 °C (50 °F). In addition, the location in question must not be [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") or [arid](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate"). The cooler *Dfb*, *Dwb*, and *Dsb* subtypes are also known as [hemiboreal](/wiki/Hemiboreal "Hemiboreal") climates. Although amount of snowfall is not a factor used in defining the humid continental climate, snow during the winter in this type of climate is almost a guarantee, either intermittently throughout the winter months near the southern or coastal margins, or persistently throughout the winter months elsewhere in the climate zone.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Hot-summer humid continental climate](/w/index.php?title=Hot-summer_humid_continental_climate&redirect=no "Hot-summer humid continental climate"))

Category in the Köppen climate classification system

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Koppen_World_Map_Dfa_Dwa_Dsa_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png/500px-Koppen_World_Map_Dfa_Dwa_Dsa_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen_World_Map_Dfa_Dwa_Dsa_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png)

*Humid continental climate* worldwide, utilizing the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")

Dsa

Dsb

Dwa

[Dwb](#Koppen_Dwb)

[Dfa](#Koppen_Dfa)

[Dfb](#Koppen_Dfb)

A **humid continental climate** is a [climatic](/wiki/Climate "Climate") region defined by Russo-German [climatologist](/wiki/Climatologist "Climatologist") [Wladimir Köppen](/wiki/Wladimir_K%C3%B6ppen "Wladimir Köppen") in 1900,[[1]](#cite_note-2014revisit-1) typified by four distinct seasons and large seasonal temperature differences, with warm to hot (and often humid) summers, and cold (sometimes severely cold in the northern areas) and snowy winters. Precipitation is usually distributed throughout the year, but often these regions do have dry seasons. The definition of this climate in terms of temperature is as follows: the mean temperature of the coldest month must be below 0 °C (32.0 °F) or −3 °C (26.6 °F) depending on the isotherm,[[2]](#cite_note-kottek2006-2) and there must be at least four months whose mean temperatures are at or above 10 °C (50 °F). In addition, the location in question must not be [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") or [arid](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate"). The cooler *Dfb*, *Dwb*, and *Dsb* subtypes are also known as [hemiboreal](/wiki/Hemiboreal "Hemiboreal") climates. Although amount of snowfall is not a factor used in defining the humid continental climate, snow during the winter in this type of climate is almost a guarantee, either intermittently throughout the winter months near the southern or coastal margins, or persistently throughout the winter months elsewhere in the climate zone.

Humid continental climates are generally found between latitudes [40° N](/wiki/40th_parallel_north "40th parallel north") and [60° N](/wiki/60th_parallel_north "60th parallel north"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) within the central and northeastern portions of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"). Occasionally, they can also be found at higher elevations above other more temperate climate types. They are rare in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), limited to isolated high altitude locations, due to the larger ocean area at that latitude, smaller land mass, and the consequent greater maritime moderation.

In the Northern Hemisphere, some of the humid continental climates, typically in around [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido"), [Sakhalin Island](/wiki/Sakhalin_Island "Sakhalin Island"), northeastern mainland [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)") are closer to the sea and heavily maritime-influenced and comparable to [oceanic climates](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"), with relatively cool summers, significant year-round precipitation (including high amounts of snow) and winters being just below the freezing mark (too cold for such a classification).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

More extreme and inland humid continental climates, sometimes known as "hyper-continental" climates, are found in northeast [China](/wiki/China "China"), southern [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), most of the southern interior of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), and the [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest"), where temperatures in the winter resemble those of adjacent [subarctic climates](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") (with long, drier, generally very cold winters) but have longer and generally warmer summers (in occasional cases, hot summers). A more moderate variety, found in places like [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu"), east-central China, the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"), parts of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), parts of southern [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), much of the [American Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), and the [Northeast US](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), the climate combines hotter summer maxima and greater humidity (similar to those found in adjacent [humid subtropical climates](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate")) and moderately cold winters and more intermittent snow cover (averaging somewhat below freezing, too cold for a more temperate classification), and is less extreme than the most inland hyper-continental variety.

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humid_continental_climate&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Odori_Park_Sapporo_Snow_Festival_2007.JPG/250px-Odori_Park_Sapporo_Snow_Festival_2007.JPG)](/wiki/File:Odori_Park_Sapporo_Snow_Festival_2007.JPG)

The snowy city of [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"), Japan, has a humid continental climate ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*).

Using the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), a climate is classified as humid continental when the temperature of the coldest month is below 0 °C [32.0 °F] or −3 °C [26.6 °F] and there must be at least four months whose mean temperatures are at or above 10 °C (50 °F).[[5]](#cite_note-Peel-5) These temperatures were not arbitrary. In Europe, the −3 °C (27 °F) average temperature [isotherm](/wiki/Isotherm_(contour_line) "Isotherm (contour line)") (line of equal temperature) was near the southern extent of winter [snowpack](/wiki/Snowpack "Snowpack"). In the United States, it is more common to use the 0 °C [32.0 °F] isotherm instead. The 10 °C (50 °F) average temperature was found to be roughly the minimum temperature necessary for tree reproduction and growth.[[6]](#cite_note-MetToday-6) Wide temperature ranges are common within this climate zone.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Second letter in the classification symbol defines seasonal rainfall as follows:[[5]](#cite_note-Peel-5)

* *s*: A dry summer—the driest month in the high-sun half of the year (April to September in the Northern Hemisphere, October to March in the Southern Hemisphere) has less than 30 millimetres (1.18 in)/40 millimetres (1.57 in) of rainfall and has exactly or less than 1⁄3 the precipitation of the wettest month in the low-sun half of the year (October to March in the Northern Hemisphere, April to September in the Southern Hemisphere).
* *w*: A dry winter—the driest month in the low-sun half of the year has exactly or less than one‑tenth of the precipitation found in the wettest month in the summer half of the year.
* *f*: No dry season—does not meet either of the alternative specifications above; precipitation and humidity are often high year-round.

while the third letter denotes the extent of summer heat:[[5]](#cite_note-Peel-5)

* *a*: Hot summer, warmest month averages at least 22 °C (71.6 °F),
* *b*: Warm summer, warmest month averages below 22 °C (71.6 °F) but at least four months averages above 10 °C (50.0 °F).

### Associated precipitation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humid_continental_climate&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Associated precipitation")]

Within North America, moisture within this climate regime is supplied by the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") and adjacent western subtropical [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic "Atlantic").[[8]](#cite_note-env-8) [Precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") is relatively well distributed year-round in many areas with this climate (*f*), while others may see a marked reduction in wintry precipitation,[[6]](#cite_note-MetToday-6) which increases the chances of a wintertime [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") (*w*).[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Snowfall](/wiki/Snow "Snow") occurs in all areas with a humid continental climate and in many such places is more common than [rain](/wiki/Rain "Rain") during the height of winter. In places with sufficient wintertime precipitation, the snow cover is often deep. Most summer rainfall occurs during [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm"),[[6]](#cite_note-MetToday-6) and in North America and Asia an occasional [tropical cyclone](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") (or the remnants thereof). Though [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity") levels are often high in locations with humid continental climates, the "humid" designation means that the climate is not dry enough to be classified as [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") or [arid](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate").

### Vegetation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humid_continental_climate&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Vegetation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Vermont_fall_foliage_hogback_mountain.JPG/250px-Vermont_fall_foliage_hogback_mountain.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vermont_fall_foliage_hogback_mountain.JPG)

Mixed forest in [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") during autumn

By definition, [forests](/wiki/Forest "Forest") thrive within this climate. [Biomes](/wiki/Biome "Biome") within this climate regime include temperate woodlands, temperate grasslands, temperate deciduous or evergreen forests,[[8]](#cite_note-env-8) coniferous forests, and coniferous swamps.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Within wetter areas, [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple"), [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce"), [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine"), [fir](/wiki/Fir "Fir"), and [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") can be found. Fall foliage is noted during the autumn of deciduous forests.[[6]](#cite_note-MetToday-6)

### Neighboring climates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humid_continental_climate&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Neighboring climates")]

In the poleward direction, these climates transition into [subarctic climates](/wiki/Subarctic_climates "Subarctic climates") featuring short summers (and usually very cold winters) allowing only [conifer](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") trees. Moving equatorword, the hot-summer continental climates grade into [humid subtropical climates](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (chiefly in North America and Asia) while the warm-summer continental climates grade into [oceanic climates](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (chiefly in Europe), both of which have milder winters where average temperatures stay above 0 °C (or -3 °C). Some continental climates with lower precipitation (chiefly in Central Asia and the Western United States) grade into [semi-arid climates](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") with similar temperatures but low precipitation.

## Hot summer subtype

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humid_continental_climate&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hot summer subtype")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Koppen_World_Map_Dfa_Dwa_Dsa.png/250px-Koppen_World_Map_Dfa_Dwa_Dsa.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen_World_Map_Dfa_Dwa_Dsa.png)

Regions with hot-summer humid continental climates

A hot summer version of a continental climate features an average temperature of at least 22 °C (71.6 °F) in its warmest month.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Since these regimes are restricted to the Northern Hemisphere, the warmest month is usually July or August. High temperatures during the warmest month tend to be in the high 20s to low 30s °C (80s °F), while average January afternoon temperatures are near or well below freezing. Frost-free periods typically last 4 to 7 months in this climate regime.[[6]](#cite_note-MetToday-6)

Within [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), this climate includes portions of the central and eastern United States from east of [100°W](/wiki/100th_meridian_west "100th meridian west") to south of about the [44°N](/wiki/44th_parallel_north "44th parallel north") to the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). Precipitation increases further eastward in this zone and is less seasonally uniform in the west. The western states of the western [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (namely [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), parts of southern [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), most of [Lincoln County](/wiki/Lincoln_County,_Washington "Lincoln County, Washington") in [Eastern Washington](/wiki/Eastern_Washington "Eastern Washington"), parts of [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), parts of [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), isolated parts of northern [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), western [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), and parts of western [North](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota") and [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota")) have thermal regimes which fit the *Dfa* climate type, but are quite dry, and are generally grouped with the steppe (*BSk*) climates.

In the eastern and Midwestern United States, [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"), southern [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), southern [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), southern [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), southern [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), most of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") and [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), and eastern [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") fall into the hot-summer humid continental climate. In Canada, this climate type exists only over portions of Southern [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and the island of Montreal.

In the Eastern Hemisphere, this climate regime is found within interior [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") and east-central Asia. Within Europe, the *Dfa* climate type is present near the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") in southern [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), the [Southern Federal District](/wiki/Southern_Federal_District "Southern Federal District") of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), southern [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), parts of southern [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"),[[12]](#cite_note-Fundy-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Explore-13) but tends to be drier and can be even semi-arid in these places. In East Asia, this climate exhibits a [monsoonal](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") tendency with much higher precipitation in summer than in winter, and due to the effects of the strong [Siberian High](/wiki/Siberian_High "Siberian High") much colder winter temperatures than similar latitudes around the world, however with lower snowfall, the exception being western Japan with its heavy snowfall.

[Tōhoku](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku "Tōhoku") on the western coast of Japan also has a climate with Köppen classification *Dfa*, but is wetter even than that part of North America with this climate type. A variant which has dry winters and hence relatively lower snowfall with monsoonal type summer rainfall is to be found in northern [China](/wiki/China "China") including [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria") and parts of [North China](/wiki/North_China "North China"), far East of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), and over much of the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula"); it has the Köppen classification *Dwa*. Much of [central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), [northwestern China](/wiki/Northwestern_China "Northwestern China"), and southern [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia") has a thermal regime similar to that of the *Dfa* climate type, but these regions receive so little precipitation that they are more often classified as steppes (*BSk*) or deserts (*BWk*).

*Dsa* climates are rare; they are generally restricted to elevated areas adjacent to mid-latitude [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") regions with a *Csa* climate well inland to ensure hot summers and cold winters. They are generally found in the highly elevated areas of south-eastern Turkey ([Hakkâri](/wiki/Hakk%C3%A2ri_Province "Hakkâri Province")), north-western Iran, northern Iraq, parts of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), parts of the [High Atlas](/wiki/High_Atlas "High Atlas") mountain range in central Morocco and parts of the [Intermountain West](/wiki/Intermountain_West "Intermountain West") in the United States.

This climate zone does not exist at all in the Southern Hemisphere, where the continents either do not penetrate low enough in latitude or [taper too much](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone") to have any place that gets the combination of snowy winters and hot summers. Marine influences are very strong around 40°S and such preclude *Dfa*, *Dwa*, and *Dsa* climates from existing in the southern hemisphere.

## Warm summer subtype

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humid_continental_climate&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Warm summer subtype")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Koppen_World_Map_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png/250px-Koppen_World_Map_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen_World_Map_Dfb_Dwb_Dsb.png)

Regions with warm-summer humid continental climates

Also known as **[hemiboreal climate](/wiki/Hemiboreal "Hemiboreal")**, areas featuring this subtype of the continental climate have an average temperature in the warmest month below 22 °C (72 °F). Summer high temperatures in this zone typically average between 21–28 °C (70–82 °F) during the daytime and the average temperatures in the coldest month are generally well or far below the −3 °C (27 °F) (or 0 °C (32.0 °F)) isotherm. Frost-free periods typically last 3–5 months. Heat spells lasting over a week are rare.[[6]](#cite_note-MetToday-6)

The warm summer version of the humid continental climate covers a much larger area than the hot subtype. In North America, the climate zone covers from about [42°N](/wiki/42nd_parallel_north "42nd parallel north") to [50°N](/wiki/50th_parallel_north "50th parallel north") latitude mostly east of [100°W](/wiki/100th_meridian_west "100th meridian west"), including parts of [Southern Ontario](/wiki/Southern_Ontario "Southern Ontario"), the southern half of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), [The Maritimes](/wiki/The_Maritimes "The Maritimes"), and [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)"), as well as the northern United States from eastern [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota") east to [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"). However, it can be found as far north as [54°N](/wiki/54th_parallel_north "54th parallel north"), and further west in the Canadian [Prairie Provinces](/wiki/Prairie_Provinces "Prairie Provinces")[[20]](#cite_note-20) and below [40°N](/wiki/40th_parallel_north "40th parallel north") in the high [Appalachians](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"). In Europe, this subtype reaches its most northerly latitude in [Bodø](/wiki/Bod%C3%B8 "Bodø") at the [67°N](/wiki/67th_parallel_north "67th parallel north").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

High-altitude locations such as [Flagstaff, Arizona](/wiki/Flagstaff,_Arizona "Flagstaff, Arizona"), [Aspen, Colorado](/wiki/Aspen,_Colorado "Aspen, Colorado") and [Los Alamos, New Mexico](/wiki/Los_Alamos,_New_Mexico "Los Alamos, New Mexico") in the western United States exhibit local *Dfb* climates. The south-central and southwestern [Prairie Provinces](/wiki/Prairie_Provinces "Prairie Provinces") also fits the *Dfb* criteria from a thermal profile, but because of semi-arid precipitation portions of it are grouped into the *BSk* category.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), it is found in much of [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"): [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") (in the east and southeast part of the country), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") (generally below 700 m (2,297 ft)), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") (generally above 100 m (328 ft)), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") (mostly [Slavonia](/wiki/Slavonia "Slavonia") region), in much of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"): [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") (the whole country except the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") coast), [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") (mostly central part of [European Russia](/wiki/European_Russia "European Russia")), south and central parts of the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") not bathed by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") or [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"): [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") (historical regions of [Svealand](/wiki/Svealand "Svealand") and [Götaland](/wiki/G%C3%B6taland "Götaland")), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") (south end, including the [three largest cities](/wiki/List_of_Finnish_municipalities "List of Finnish municipalities")),[[13]](#cite_note-Explore-13) [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") (most populated area),[[5]](#cite_note-Peel-5) all [Baltic States](/wiki/Baltic_States "Baltic States"): [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") and also in parts of: [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") (generally above 100 m (328 ft)), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and in the [Cairngorm Mountains](/wiki/Cairngorm_Mountains "Cairngorm Mountains") of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), (generally above 100 m (328 ft)).[[12]](#cite_note-Fundy-12)[[23]](#cite_note-MGM-23) It has little warming or precipitation effects from the northern Atlantic.[[13]](#cite_note-Explore-13) The cool summer subtype is marked by mild summers, long cold winters and less [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology) "Precipitation (meteorology)") than the hot summer subtype; however, short periods of extreme heat are not uncommon. Northern [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") has a similar climate.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In Asia, this climate type is found in northern [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), southern [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), parts of [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), northern [China](/wiki/China "China"), and highland elevations in the [Koreas](/wiki/Korea "Korea"). Like its hot-summer counterpart, these climates are typically dry in the winter and bitterly cold due to the [Siberian High](/wiki/Siberian_High "Siberian High") (often with winter temperatures comparable to their nearby [subarctic climates](/wiki/Subarctic_climates "Subarctic climates")), while summers are warm and long enough to avoid classification as a subarctic climate.

In the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), it exists in well-defined areas only in the [Southern Alps](/wiki/Southern_Alps "Southern Alps") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) in the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") in [Kiandra, New South Wales](/wiki/Kiandra,_New_South_Wales "Kiandra, New South Wales")[[26]](#cite_note-26) and the [Andes Mountains](/wiki/Andes_Mountains "Andes Mountains") of [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") and [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Use in climate modeling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Humid_continental_climate&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Use in climate modeling")]

Since climate regimes tend to be dominated by vegetation of one region with relatively homogenous ecology, those that project [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") remap their results in the form of climate regimes as an alternative way to explain expected changes.[[1]](#cite_note-2014revisit-1)
